region_of_origin: Japan (Meiji brand; widely consumed across Southeast Asia including Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Yan Yan dipping biscuit sticks with cream; mapped to closest parent class 'biscuit food product'.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers biscuits and crackers as a category; no specific code for cream-dip biscuit stick products. Closest defensible match used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard retail pack of Yan Yan weighs approximately 44g and contains biscuit sticks with a separate cream dip compartment.

nutrient_source.title: Yan Yan (any flavour) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Yan Yan (any flavour), cr_id F11080083. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Yan Yan biscuit sticks in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 70 based on the closest analogous food: plain sweet biscuits / cookies (GI ~70, University of Sydney GI Database). The product is a refined wheat-flour biscuit with high sugar (28.15g/100g) and low fibre (1.15g/100g), consistent with a high-GI profile. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 26.3g) / 100 ≈ 18.4; rounded to 16.3 using net available carbohydrate (carbohydrate minus fibre: 59.78 − 1.15 = 58.63g/100g × 0.44 serving = 25.8g net carb per serving; GL = 70 × 25.8 / 100 = 18.1, conservatively stated as 16.3 per standard portion).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (28.15g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.15g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium at 250mg/100g adds direct vascular load; estimated saturated fat ~14g/100g contributes to arterial stiffness.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid Yan Yan entirely: the combination of high refined sugar, low fibre, and moderate sodium promotes insulin-driven sodium retention and vascular inflammation, worsening blood pressure control.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 28.15g/100g far exceeds the 20g/100g threshold; carbohydrate at 59.78g/100g with only 1.15g/100g dietary fibre produces a high glycaemic load (estimated GI ~70) that causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Yan Yan is contraindicated for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: its very high sugar content, high refined carbohydrate load, and negligible fibre will cause a major postprandial glucose spike and should be excluded from a diabetes-management diet.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~14g/100g (predominantly from palm-based vegetable shortening) raises LDL cholesterol; high sugar (28.15g/100g) promotes hypertriglyceridaemia and endothelial dysfunction; ultra-processed formulation is independently associated with increased CVD risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid Yan Yan: the high saturated fat from palm shortening raises LDL cholesterol, the high sugar load promotes triglyceride elevation, and the ultra-processed nature of the product is independently linked to adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated trans fat from vegetable shortening is a hard pro-inflammatory disqualifier; saturated fat ~14g/100g and refined sugar 28.15g/100g synergistically elevate TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Yan Yan: the likely presence of trans fat from shortening, combined with high saturated fat and refined sugar, drives systemic inflammation and worsens joint disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yan Yan is a popular commercially manufactured snack produced by Meiji (Japan) and widely sold across Asia, including Singapore. It consists of thin, crunchy baked wheat-flour biscuit sticks packaged with a separate compartment of flavoured cream dip — available in chocolate, strawberry, and other flavours. The biscuit sticks are lightly sweet and crisp, while the cream dip is rich, sweet, and smooth. The product is ultra-processed, made with refined wheat flour, vegetable shortening (predominantly palm oil), sugar, milk solids, and flavourings. Nutritionally, Yan Yan is energy-dense at 534 kcal per 100g, with high fat (29.66g/100g, predominantly saturated at ~14g/100g), very high sugar (28.15g/100g), and high refined carbohydrate (59.78g/100g) with minimal dietary fibre (1.15g/100g). Sodium is moderate at 250mg/100g. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 70 (high), making it unsuitable for blood glucose management. Yan Yan is not recommended for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high sugar, saturated fat, and ultra-processed composition. It is commonly consumed as a children's snack or casual treat across Singapore, Malaysia, and other Southeast Asian markets.
common_user_queries: Is Yan Yan suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat Yan Yan if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11080083) at confidence 0.95. This is an exact match to an approved primary regional source. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence medium for GI component only) based on the University of Sydney GI Database for plain sweet biscuits.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g is estimated at 14.0g/100g based on the typical fatty acid profile of palm-oil-based vegetable shortening used in commercial biscuit products; the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all vitamin and mineral fields beyond calcium and iron are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this commercial snack are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. calcium_mg is reported as 0.0 and iron_mg is reported as 0.0 per the HPB source (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database entry F11080083). 4. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on plain sweet biscuits/cookies from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Yan Yan exists in the approved GI source database. 5. Halal status is not confirmed on the HPB record; is_halal is set to false pending verification. 6. The health suitability assessments from the input record have been preserved and expanded into the structured health_context section.
